topic: Location, Extent & Physiographic Divisions of India

questions.text: The Standard Meridian of India is 82°30'E. Through which city does this meridian pass?
options: Allahabad (Prayagraj)
Varanasi
Mirzapur
Lucknow
questions.explanation: The Standard Meridian of India (82°30'E) passes through Mirzapur, Uttar Pradesh — which is why IST (Indian Standard Time = UTC+5:30) is sometimes called the 'Mirzapur time.' This meridian was chosen because it is approximately midway between India's easternmost (97°25'E) and westernmost (68°7'E) longitudes, and 82°30' is a convenient fraction that gives exactly UTC+5:30. Prayagraj (Allahabad) is nearby and often confused with Mirzapur — they are in the same region of UP but the meridian specifically passes through Mirzapur, not Allahabad city. Varanasi and Lucknow are both in UP but not on 82°30'E.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Standard Meridian of India (82°30'E, Mirzapur) is one of the most frequently tested geographical facts

questions.text: Through how many Indian states does the Tropic of Cancer (23°30'N) pass, and which of the following lists them correctly?
options: 7 states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Mizoram
8 states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram
8 states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram
9 states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Odisha, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram
questions.explanation: The Tropic of Cancer (23°30'N) passes through exactly 8 Indian states, from west to east: Gujarat → Rajasthan → Madhya Pradesh → Chhattisgarh → Jharkhand → West Bengal → Tripura → Mizoram. The most common errors: (1) Including Odisha — Odisha is too far south and east; the Tropic misses it; (2) Forgetting Tripura — it comes between West Bengal and Mizoram; (3) Counting only 7 states by omitting either Tripura or Chhattisgarh. Mnemonic to remember all 8 (west to east): 'Go Rajput Men, Carefully Join West TM' (Gujarat, Rajasthan, MP, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Tropic of Cancer states (8 states, specific list) is one of the most tested location facts; Odisha is the classic wrong inclusion

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct rank of India by area among the world's countries?
options: 5th largest
6th largest
7th largest
8th largest
questions.explanation: India is the 7th largest country in the world by area (3.28 million sq km / 32,87,263 sq km). The six larger countries are: (1) Russia (~17.1 million sq km), (2) Canada (~10 million sq km), (3) USA (~9.8 million sq km), (4) China (~9.6 million sq km), (5) Brazil (~8.5 million sq km), (6) Australia (~7.7 million sq km), (7) India (~3.28 million sq km). India accounts for approximately 2.4% of the world's total land area. A common confusion: students sometimes rank India as 6th or 8th. The sequence Russia-Canada-USA-China-Brazil-Australia-India must be memorised in order.
questions.source: SSC CGL — India's rank by area (7th largest) is a standard identification question; the six larger countries and their order are also tested

questions.text: What is the correct sequence of sub-divisions of the Northern Plains from the foothills of the Himalayas going southward?
options: Terai → Bhabar → Bangar → Khadar
Bhabar → Terai → Khadar → Bangar
Bhabar → Terai → Bangar → Khadar
Bangar → Khadar → Bhabar → Terai
questions.explanation: The correct north-to-south sequence of the Northern Plains' sub-zones is: Bhabar → Terai → Bangar → Khadar. (1) Bhabar: immediately south of the Shiwaliks; pebbles and gravel; rivers disappear underground here; 8-16 km wide. (2) Terai: south of Bhabar; fine alluvium; underground rivers re-emerge as marshy, waterlogged ground; originally forested (Terai wildlife zones). (3) Bangar: old alluvium ('older floodplain'); slightly elevated; calcareous deposits (kankar); above normal flood level; better for settlement. (4) Khadar: new alluvium; lower ground along active river channels; floods regularly; highly fertile due to fresh silt deposition. The Bangar-Khadar sequence (old vs. new alluvium, higher vs. lower ground) is the most commonly confused pair.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Bhabar-Terai-Bangar-Khadar sequence (north to south) is a frequently tested ordering question; Bangar vs. Khadar distinction (old/elevated vs. new/flooded) is the specific trap

questions.text: What is India's longest international land border, and with which country does India share its shortest international land border?
options: Longest: Pakistan (~3,323 km); Shortest: Nepal (~1,751 km)
Longest: China (~3,488 km); Shortest: Bhutan (~699 km)
Longest: Bangladesh (~4,156 km); Shortest: Afghanistan (~106 km)
Longest: Bangladesh (~4,156 km); Shortest: Sri Lanka (sea border, so technically zero land border)
questions.explanation: India's longest international land border is with Bangladesh (~4,156 km) — Bangladesh is surrounded by India on three sides (north, west, east), which accounts for the exceptionally long border despite Bangladesh being a much smaller country. India's shortest international land border is with Afghanistan (~106 km), which exists via the Pakistan-administered Kashmir (PoK) corridor — making it disputed. The border lengths approximately: Bangladesh ~4,156 km > China ~3,488 km > Pakistan ~3,323 km > Nepal ~1,751 km > Myanmar ~1,643 km > Bhutan ~699 km > Afghanistan ~106 km. Sri Lanka has NO land border with India — it is separated by the Palk Strait and Gulf of Mannar (sea border only). The Bangladesh vs. Pakistan confusion is the most common error — Pakistan features more prominently in news, but Bangladesh's border is actually longer.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because longest (Bangladesh) vs. shortest (Afghanistan) border is a standard fill-in-the-blank that students get wrong by assuming Pakistan is longest; the Sri Lanka no-land-border fact is also tested as a trap

questions.text: What is the difference between the southernmost point of India and the southernmost point of the Indian mainland?
options: There is no difference — Kanyakumari IS the southernmost point of India in all senses

Southernmost point of India = Indira Point (Great Nicobar Island, ~6°45'N); southernmost point of the Indian mainland = Kanyakumari/Cape Comorin (~8°4'N); Indira Point was partially submerged by the 2004 tsunami
Southernmost point of India = Minicoy Island (Lakshadweep); southernmost point of the mainland = Kanyakumari
Southernmost point of India = Point Calimere (Tamil Nadu coast), which extends slightly further south than Kanyakumari
questions.explanation: Two different southernmost points: (1) Southernmost point of the Indian MAINLAND = Cape Comorin (Kanyakumari), Tamil Nadu, at approximately 8°4'N — where the Bay of Bengal, Arabian Sea, and Indian Ocean meet; (2) Southernmost point of INDIA (including islands) = Indira Point, on Great Nicobar Island in the Andaman & Nicobar Islands, at approximately 6°45'N. Indira Point was partially submerged during the 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami — the lighthouse there sank into the sea. Questions that ask 'southernmost point of India' expect Indira Point; questions that specify 'mainland' expect Kanyakumari. Both carry the name 'Indira' — Indira Point in the south, Indira Col in the north (northernmost). Minicoy is Lakshadweep's southernmost island but not India's southernmost point.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because 'southernmost point of India' questions appear regularly and the Kanyakumari vs. Indira Point distinction is the classic trap; the 2004 tsunami connection to Indira Point is an additional detail tested in harder papers

questions.text: Why are the Western Ghats described as 'continuous' while the Eastern Ghats are 'discontinuous'? What is the geographic consequence of this difference?
options: Western Ghats are continuous because they formed from volcanic rock; Eastern Ghats are discontinuous because they formed from sedimentary rock that eroded faster

Western Ghats run as an unbroken range parallel to the west coast (with only the Palakkad Gap as a major break), acting as a continuous barrier to the southwest monsoon; Eastern Ghats are broken by east-flowing rivers (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery) that cut through them, making them discontinuous — this also means the east coast has rich river deltas while the west coast has backwaters and lagoons instead
Western Ghats are called continuous because they are mapped as a continuous line on political maps; Eastern Ghats are called discontinuous because they straddle multiple state boundaries
Western Ghats are higher and thus appear continuous; Eastern Ghats are lower and thus their peaks appear as isolated hills rather than a connected range
questions.explanation: The continuous vs. discontinuous distinction has a clear geographic basis: (1) Western Ghats form an almost unbroken wall running ~1,600 km along the west coast. The only significant gap is the Palakkad Gap (Palghat Gap) in Kerala/Tamil Nadu. This continuity means they act as a barrier to the southwest monsoon — the western (windward) side gets 2,000-3,000 mm+ of rainfall; the eastern (leeward) side is in a pronounced rain shadow. (2) Eastern Ghats are cut through repeatedly by major east-flowing rivers — the Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, and Cauvery all break through the Eastern Ghats before reaching the Bay of Bengal. This is why the east coast has major river deltas (fertile, flat ground built from river sediment) while the west coast lacks comparable deltas — west-coast rivers (Narmada, Tapi) are short and flow through rift valleys, not building large deltas. The rain shadow effect of the continuous Western Ghats and the deltaic coast consequence of discontinuous Eastern Ghats are both standard exam topics.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the continuous/discontinuous distinction appears as a one-line fact but the geographic consequences (rain shadow, west coast backwaters vs. east coast deltas) are the analytical content tested in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: India's Peninsular Plateau is described as a piece of 'Gondwana land.' What does this mean, and why does it explain the Plateau's geological characteristics?
options: Gondwana land was a mythological Hindu kingdom described in the Puranas that corresponds to the geographical extent of the Deccan Plateau

Gondwana was the ancient supercontinent of the southern hemisphere that existed ~200-300 million years ago, comprising what is now South America, Africa, Arabia, India (the Peninsular portion), Antarctica, and Australia; the Peninsular Plateau is a fragment of this old craton — which is why it has ancient, stable rocks (not folded or volcanic in the Himalayan sense), rich mineral deposits, and a geological stability that the young Himalayas lack
Gondwana land was the name given by British geologists to the coal-bearing formations of central India — the 'Gondwana formation' refers only to coal deposits, not the whole plateau
Gondwana was the name of a medieval kingdom in Madhya Pradesh; calling the plateau 'Gondwana land' is a historical tribute to that kingdom, not a geological statement
questions.explanation: Gondwana (named after the Gond people of central India, where the geological series was first studied) was the ancient southern supercontinent that existed approximately 200-300 million years ago. It comprised what is now South America, Africa, Arabia, the Indian subcontinent's southern/older portion (the Peninsular Plateau), Antarctica, and Australia. When Gondwana broke up, the Indian plate drifted northward and collided with the Eurasian plate — forming the Himalayas. The Peninsular Plateau is the remnant of Gondwana craton: it is geologically ancient (Archean and Proterozoic rocks, over 600 million years old), tectonically stable, not subject to earthquakes like the young Himalayas, and rich in metallic minerals (iron ore, manganese, bauxite, copper, gold) because ancient rocks concentrate heavy metals. The Deccan Trap basalt is a later addition (~65 million years ago, volcanic outpourings) but the underlying plateau rocks are Gondwana-era. This geological age is why the Peninsular Plateau is fundamentally different in character from the Himalayas.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because 'Gondwana land' appears as a label in textbooks but the actual meaning (ancient southern supercontinent, plate tectonics, why it makes the Plateau mineral-rich and stable) is the analytical content that appears in higher-difficulty geography questions

questions.text: Barren Island is notable in Indian geography for what reason, and where is it located?
options: Barren Island is the smallest inhabited island in India, located in Lakshadweep, notable for having no freshwater source
Barren Island is India's only active volcano, located in the Andaman Sea (part of the Andaman & Nicobar Islands); it last erupted in 2017 and periodically shows volcanic activity
Barren Island is the uninhabited island closest to Sri Lanka, located near the Palk Strait, notable as a former British naval base
Barren Island is the name of the central island of the Lakshadweep group, notable for having no coral reef despite being surrounded by atolls
questions.explanation: Barren Island is India's only active volcano — located in the Andaman Sea, approximately 135 km northeast of Port Blair, in the Andaman & Nicobar Islands. It is part of the volcanic arc that runs through the Andaman and Nicobar chain (connected to the Indonesian volcanic belt). Barren Island has erupted multiple times in documented history — 1787, 1795, 1803-1804, 1991 (after a long dormant period), 1994-95, 2005 (after the 2004 tsunami), 2010, 2017. A companion volcano — Narcondam Island, also in the Andamans — is classified as dormant. These are the only two volcanoes in India. The broader volcanic chain they belong to runs through Indonesia (Krakatoa, etc.) and up through the Andamans into Myanmar. Lakshadweep has no volcanoes — it is entirely coral (atolls).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Barren Island appears as a one-line 'only active volcano' fact; its location (Andaman Sea, not near Sri Lanka or Lakshadweep), its last eruption dates, and its distinction from Narcondam (dormant) are the specific tested details in harder papers

questions.text: What is the 'Ten Degree Channel' and why is it geographically significant in the context of India's island territories?
options: The Ten Degree Channel is the international maritime boundary between India and Sri Lanka in the Palk Strait, located at 10°N latitude

The Ten Degree Channel (approximately 150 km wide) separates the Andaman Islands from the Nicobar Islands at approximately 10°N latitude; it is significant because it marks the administrative and ecological boundary between the two groups — the Andamans and Nicobars have distinct wildlife communities partly because of this water barrier
The Ten Degree Channel is the deepest point in the Bay of Bengal, located at 10°N, where the oceanic depth exceeds 10,000 metres
The Ten Degree Channel separates Lakshadweep from the Indian mainland at 10°N — the channel is 10 degrees of longitude wide
questions.explanation: The Ten Degree Channel (also called the 10° Channel) is a waterway approximately 150 km wide that separates the Andaman Islands (to the north) from the Nicobar Islands (to the south), located at approximately 10°N latitude. It is geographically significant because: (1) It is the boundary between the two island groups — administratively, both are part of the Union Territory of Andaman & Nicobar Islands, but geographically distinct; (2) The channel is deep enough to create a faunal barrier — the wildlife on Andaman islands differs significantly from Nicobar islands; (3) It also marks an important naval and maritime boundary in the Bay of Bengal. Other notable channels/passages: Duncan Passage (between South Andaman and Little Andaman), Eight Degree Channel (between Lakshadweep and Maldives), Palk Strait (between India and Sri Lanka). The 'Ten Degree' name comes from the approximate latitude (10°N), not from its width or depth.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the Ten Degree Channel, Duncan Passage, Eight Degree Channel are all tested but students often confuse which separates what; this channel (Andaman from Nicobar at 10°N) is the most frequently tested of the three

questions.text:
Match the physiographic division with its key characteristic:
1. Northern Mountains       A. Oldest landmass; part of Gondwana; mineral-rich
2. Northern Plains          B. Bhabar-Terai-Bangar-Khadar zones; most fertile land
3. Peninsular Plateau       C. Young fold mountains; three parallel ranges; still rising
4. Coastal Plains           D. West = narrow/straight; East = wide/deltaic
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Northern Mountains → C. Young fold mountains (Himalayas formed ~50 million years ago when Indo-Australian plate collided with Eurasian plate); three parallel ranges (Himadri, Himachal, Shiwaliks); still tectonically active and rising. (2) Northern Plains → B. Bhabar (pebble zone at foothills, rivers disappear) → Terai (marshy, rivers reappear) → Bangar (old alluvium, elevated) → Khadar (new alluvium, fertile, floods); most densely populated and fertile region. (3) Peninsular Plateau → A. Oldest landmass (Archean/Gondwana origin, 600+ million years old); tectonically stable; rich in metallic minerals (iron ore, manganese, bauxite, copper). (4) Coastal Plains → D. West coast = narrow, straight (Konkan, Malabar, no major deltas); East coast = wide, deltaic (Mahanadi, Godavari, Krishna, Cauvery deltas on Coromandel/Northern Circars coasts).
questions.source: Physiographic division matching is the most frequently tested geography question pattern for this topic — all four divisions with their key characteristics are tested as matching or fill-in-the-blank

questions.text: Which of the following statements about the Lakshadweep and Andaman & Nicobar Islands is INCORRECT?
options: Lakshadweep consists of coral islands (atolls) while the Andaman & Nicobar Islands are of volcanic and sedimentary origin
The capital of Lakshadweep is Kavaratti; the capital of Andaman & Nicobar Islands is Port Blair
Lakshadweep is located in the Arabian Sea; the Andaman & Nicobar Islands are located in the Bay of Bengal
Barren Island (India's only active volcano) is located in Lakshadweep, while Narcondam (dormant volcano) is in the Andaman Islands
questions.explanation: The INCORRECT statement is option D. Barren Island (India's only active volcano) is located in the ANDAMAN SEA — it is part of the Andaman & Nicobar Islands, NOT Lakshadweep. Lakshadweep consists entirely of coral islands (atolls and reefs) and has no volcanoes whatsoever. Narcondam is also in the Andaman Islands (a dormant volcano), NOT in Lakshadweep. Options A, B, and C are all correct: coral (Lakshadweep) vs. volcanic/sedimentary (A&N) (A); Kavaratti and Port Blair as respective capitals (B); Arabian Sea vs. Bay of Bengal (C). The Barren Island location (Andaman vs. Lakshadweep) is the single most tested islands fact in SSC geography.
questions.source: INCORRECT statement format for island facts (Barren Island is in Andaman, NOT Lakshadweep) is the most frequently tested island question; options A-C are standard correct facts used as distractors

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct latitudinal and longitudinal extent of India?
options: Latitude: 8°4'N to 37°6'N; Longitude: 68°7'E to 97°25'E
Latitude: 6°4'N to 35°6'N; Longitude: 68°7'E to 97°25'E
Latitude: 8°4'N to 37°6'N; Longitude: 72°E to 97°E
Latitude: 10°N to 40°N; Longitude: 70°E to 98°E
questions.explanation: India's precise extent: Latitudinal: 8°4'N (Indira Point, Great Nicobar Island — the southernmost tip) to 37°6'N (Indira Col, Siachen region — the northernmost point). Longitudinal: 68°7'E (Guhar Moti, Rann of Kutch, Gujarat — westernmost) to 97°25'E (Kibithu, Arunachal Pradesh — easternmost). The key numbers to memorise: latitude starts at ~8°N (not 6°N, not 10°N) and ends at ~37°N (not 35°N, not 40°N); longitude is ~68°E to ~97°E. The north-south extent is about 3,214 km; east-west extent is about 2,933 km. The 8° starting latitude places Kanyakumari well within tropical India, while 37°N puts Kashmir/Ladakh in a temperate to cold-arid zone.
questions.source: India's latitudinal/longitudinal extent (8°4'N to 37°6'N; 68°7'E to 97°25'E) is a direct factual question tested almost every year in SSC CGL geography sections
